title_en: The Female Companions: Women in Usd al-Ghabah
content_en:
One of the most important dimensions of Usd al-Ghabah fi Ma'rifat as-Sahaba is its treatment of the female Companions — the Sahabiyyat — whose biographies form a significant portion of the final volume of the work. Ibn al-Athir's inclusion of women's biographies at this level of detail was not a departure from the earlier tradition; both Ibn Abd al-Barr in Al-Isti'ab and Ibn Sa'd in his Tabaqat had included women's biographies. But the comprehensive scope of Usd al-Ghabah meant that Ibn al-Athir gathered and organized more material on the female Companions than any previous single compilation.

The category of 'female Companion' (Sahabiyyah, plural Sahabiyyat) is defined by the same criteria applied to male Companions: a Muslim woman who saw the Prophet, peace be upon him, while believing, and who died in a state of belief. This definition encompasses an extraordinarily diverse range of women: the Prophet's wives, his daughters and female relatives, women who had been enslaved and then freed, women from wealthy merchant families, women who participated in the battles as nurses and water-carriers, women who transmitted hadith and became scholars in their own right, women known from a single incident or a single hadith, and women known through hundreds of narrations.

The Prophet's wives — the Mothers of the Believers — receive among the most detailed entries in the female companion section. Khadijah bint Khuwaylid, the first wife and first Muslim, is described as the indispensable support of the earliest phase of the prophetic mission: her wealth financed the early Muslim community, her encouragement was the first response to the revelation, and her death — in the Year of Sorrow alongside Abu Talib — left the Prophet visibly bereaved in a way that the narrations record with great care. Aisha bint Abi Bakr, the most prolific transmitter of hadith among the Prophet's wives and the source of an enormous proportion of what is known about the Prophet's private life and character, receives extensive treatment drawing on the biographical literature and the hadith collections. Ibn al-Athir navigates the sensitive historical episodes connected with Aisha — including the incident of the slander (ifk) and her role in the political conflicts after the Prophet's death — with the balance characteristic of his approach to contested historical material.

Beyond the Mothers of the Believers, Usd al-Ghabah documents the lives of women who participated actively in the early Muslim community in roles that challenge any reductive understanding of women's place in early Islam. Nusaybah bint Ka'b (Umm Umarah) was among the most celebrated warrior-Companions: she participated in the Battle of Uhud, where she fought directly to protect the Prophet when the lines broke, and sustained multiple wounds. The Prophet later said he looked left and right during the worst moments of Uhud and saw Nusaybah fighting. She also participated in later military expeditions and is remembered as one of the most courageous figures of the entire companion generation, regardless of gender.

Sumayya bint Khabbat — one of the family of Yasir — has the distinction of being the first martyr in Islam, killed by Abu Jahl when she refused to recant her faith under torture. Her entry in Usd al-Ghabah is brief in biographical detail — there was little to record beyond the central fact of her martyrdom — but enormous in significance. She stands at the beginning of a long line of those who chose death over apostasy, and her memory was kept alive through the generations precisely because her story captured the absolute seriousness with which the first Muslims held their faith.

Women scholars and transmitters occupy an important place in the biographical entries. The Prophet's granddaughter Fatimah bint Husayn, Aisha bint Sa'd, Umm al-Darda al-Kubra, and many others transmitted hadith and were consulted on matters of religious knowledge by male scholars. These entries, collectively, document the participation of women in the scholarly transmission of Islamic knowledge from the earliest period — a participation that continued through subsequent generations, as documented in the biographical dictionaries of female scholars (such as Ibn Hajar's Taqrib al-Tahdhib and al-Sakhawi's Al-Daw al-Lami').

The methodological dimension of these female companion entries is also significant. Ibn al-Athir applies his standard criteria for companion status to women exactly as he does to men: he records the chain of transmission by which each woman's companion status is established, notes disagreements about specific women's biographies, and evaluates the reliability of accounts about them with the same tools he uses elsewhere. This methodological consistency reflects the Islamic scholarly tradition's recognition that the companion status of women carried the same religious weight as that of men — their testimonies were authoritative, their narrations of hadith were accepted, and their example was normative for subsequent generations of Muslim women.

For historians of gender and religion, the female companion entries in Usd al-Ghabah provide primary source material of exceptional importance. They document women as active participants in the founding of a major world religion — as financial supporters, as warriors, as scholars, as mothers who raised the next generation of Muslims, and as sources of authoritative religious knowledge. The picture that emerges from these biographies, taken collectively, is of a prophetic community in which women's contributions were valued and recorded even in a biographical tradition dominated by male scholars.
title_id: Para Sahabat Wanita: Kaum Perempuan dalam Usd al-Ghabah
content_id:
Salah satu dimensi terpenting dari Usd al-Ghabah fi Ma'rifat as-Shahabah adalah perlakuannya terhadap para Sahabat wanita — the Shahabiyyat — yang biografinya membentuk bagian signifikan dari jilid terakhir. Ibnu al-Atsir memahami bahwa generasi Muslim pertama mencakup perempuan yang memegang peran aktif dalam kehidupan komunitas, dan mengabaikan mereka berarti merekam sejarah yang tidak lengkap.

Profil biografi para Sahabat wanita dalam Usd al-Ghabah mencakup para istri Nabi ﷺ (Ummahatul Mukminin) dengan biografi yang lebih panjang, para putri dan kerabat wanita Nabi ﷺ, wanita-wanita yang hijrah ke Madinah atau Habasyah, wanita-wanita yang ikut berbaiat kepada Nabi ﷺ di bawah Bai'at al-Aqabah atau Bai'at al-Ridwan, dan wanita-wanita yang meriwayatkan hadis — banyak dari mereka menjadi guru penting bagi generasi-generasi berikutnya.
content_ar: One of the most المهم dimensions of Usd al-Ghabah fi Ma'rifat as-الصحابة is its treatment of the female الصحابة — the Sahabiyyat — whose biographies form a المهم portion of the final volume of the work. Ibn al-Athir's inclusion of women's biographies at this level of detail was not a departure from the earlier التراث; both Ibn Abd al-Barr in Al-Isti'ab and Ibn Sa'd in his Tabaqat had included women's biographies. But the comprehensive scope of Usd al-Ghabah meant that Ibn al-Athir gathered and organized more material on the female الصحابة than any previous single compilation. The category of 'female Companion' (Sahabiyyah, plural Sahabiyyat) is defined by the same criteria applied to male الصحابة: a مسلم woman who saw النبي ﷺ, ﷺ, بينما believing, and who died in a state of الاعتقاد. This definition encompasses an extraordinarily diverse range of women: النبي ﷺ's wives, his daughters and female relatives, women who had been enslaved and then freed, women from wealthy merchant families, women who participated in the battles as nurses and water-carriers, women who transmitted الحديث and became العلماء in their own الحق, women known from a single incident or a single الحديث, and women known through hundreds of narrations. النبي ﷺ's wives — the Mothers of the المؤمنون — receive among the most detailed entries in the female companion الفصل. Khadijah bint Khuwaylid, the أولاً الزوجة and أولاً مسلم, is described as the indispensable support of the earliest phase of the prophetic mission: her الثروة financed the early مسلم الجماعة, her encouragement was the أولاً response to the الوحي, and her الموت — in the Year of Sorrow alongside Abu Talib — left النبي ﷺ visibly bereaved in a الطريق that the narrations record with great care. Aisha bint Abi Bakr, the most prolific transmitter of الحديث among النبي ﷺ's wives and the source of an enormous proportion of what is known about النبي ﷺ's private الحياة and الأخلاق, receives extensive treatment drawing on the biographical literature and the الحديث collections. Ibn al-Athir navigates the sensitive historical episodes connected with Aisha — بما في ذلك the incident of the slander (ifk) and her role in the political conflicts بعد النبي ﷺ's الموت — with the balance characteristic of his approach to contested historical material. Beyond the Mothers of the المؤمنون, Usd al-Ghabah documents the lives of women who participated actively in the early مسلم الجماعة in roles that challenge any reductive الفهم of women's place in early الإسلام. Nusaybah bint Ka'b (Umm Umarah) was among the most celebrated warrior-الصحابة: she participated in the Battle of Uhud, where she fought directly to protect النبي ﷺ عندما the lines broke, and sustained multiple wounds. النبي ﷺ later said he looked left and الحق خلال the worst moments of Uhud and saw Nusaybah fighting.
